subtitle: A Phase-Resolved Assessment of Structural Resilience and LoCT Distance

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Dynamic Threshold Positioning in U.S.–China Competition A Phase-Resolved Assessment of Structural Resilience and LoCT Distance.pdf
Dynamic Threshold Positioning in U.S.–China Competition
alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief applies an MCCM-based dynamic threshold positioning framework to U.S.–China competition, arguing that the decisive variable is not aggregate capability but each system’s distance from the loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) under sustained multi-domain pressure.
abstract: This brief examines U.S.–China competition through the lens of dynamic threshold positioning, using an MCCM-based analytical framework. Rather than comparing aggregate power, it evaluates each system’s distance to the loss-of-control threshold (LoCT) under sustained multi-domain pressure. The analysis finds that the United States operates within a high-pressure, multi-theater configuration characterized by increasing systemic exposure and coupling, resulting in a progressive compression of LoCT distance. China, by contrast, maintains a lower-exposure, controlled configuration that preserves a more stable threshold buffer. The central implication is that strategic outcomes depend less on relative strength than on relative proximity to systemic breakdown. In this context, the decisive variable is not which actor advances faster, but which system approaches the threshold first.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, systemic escalation, information conflict, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Conceptual Multi-Layer Coupled Complexity Model (MCCM) applied to U.S.–China competition and LoCT distance assessment
methodology.case_context: U.S.–China strategic competition under sustained multi-domain pressure, multi-theater engagement, alliance coordination costs, and controlled exposure dynamics
methodology.core_problem: Traditional metrics such as GDP, military expenditure, and technological output do not adequately capture systemic fragility, threshold proximity, or loss-of-control risk under sustained competitive pressure.
central_claims: Threshold proximity rather than aggregate capability is the decisive variable in contemporary great-power competition.
The United States exhibits higher volatility and declining LoCT distance as a result of sustained multi-theater engagement and rising alliance coordination costs.
China maintains lower exposure and tighter internal control, resulting in greater short-term stability and a wider LoCT buffer.
High-pressure operation and controlled low-exposure management represent distinct strategic equilibria with different risk structures.
Latent stress accumulation within low-exposure systems may erode initial stability advantages over time.

analytical_lenses.name: LoCT Distance
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates the relative buffer between a system’s current state and its loss-of-control threshold under sustained pressure.

analytical_lenses.name: Systemic Stress
analytical_lenses.description: Captures the intensity of pressure across military, economic, political, alliance, and information domains.

analytical_lenses.name: Transmission and Coupling
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses the extent to which shocks propagate across interconnected systems and domains.

analytical_lenses.name: Adaptive Capacity
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates whether a system can absorb, redistribute, and mitigate cumulative pressure before threshold compression becomes destabilizing.

analytical_lenses.name: Risk-Structure Comparison
analytical_lenses.description: Compares volatility-driven dynamics in the U.S. configuration with accumulation-driven dynamics in the China configuration.

model_variables.name: LoCT Distance
model_variables.description: Relative buffer between a system’s current state and the loss-of-control threshold.

model_variables.name: Systemic Stress
model_variables.description: Intensity of pressure across military, economic, political, and related domains.

model_variables.name: Transmission and Coupling
model_variables.description: Degree to which shocks propagate across interconnected domains and theaters.

model_variables.name: Adaptive Capacity
model_variables.description: Ability of the system to absorb, redistribute, and mitigate systemic pressure.

model_variables.name: System Configuration
model_variables.description: Institutional, alliance, geographic, and governance configuration shaping stress absorption and threshold behavior.

dimensions.name: Systemic Stress
dimensions.description: Intensity of pressure across military, economic, political, alliance, and information domains.

dimensions.name: Transmission and Coupling
dimensions.description: Extent to which shocks propagate across interconnected systems, domains, and theaters.

dimensions.name: Adaptive Capacity
dimensions.description: Ability of a system to absorb, redistribute, and mitigate pressures under sustained competition.

dimensions.name: System Structure
dimensions.description: The operating configuration that shapes stress distribution, threshold behavior, and resilience.

dimensions.name: LoCT Distance
dimensions.description: Relative buffer between current systemic state and the loss-of-control threshold.

key_findings: Threshold proximity rather than aggregate capability is the decisive variable in contemporary U.S.–China competition.
The United States operates in a high-pressure systemic equilibrium characterized by sustained multi-theater engagement, higher volatility, and gradual LoCT distance compression.
China operates in a controlled exposure configuration that limits direct high-intensity involvement and preserves a wider short-term LoCT buffer.
The U.S. risk pattern is volatility-driven, shaped by oscillation, coupling, alliance friction, and shock amplification.
China’s risk pattern is accumulation-driven, with pressures concentrated and internalized in ways that may produce delayed nonlinear adjustment.
Strategic competition is asymmetric not only in capability but in the structure of systemic risk itself.
Maintaining distance from critical thresholds is more consequential than maximizing operational expansion under sustained pressure.
Apparent stability should not be equated with safety because latent vulnerabilities may emerge abruptly.
policy_recommendations: Prioritize threshold management over expansion by maintaining distance from critical systemic thresholds.
Treat systemic coupling as a primary risk multiplier and manage cross-domain linkages alongside individual stressors.
Avoid equating stability with safety, especially in systems where latent stress accumulation may be concealed by low short-term volatility.
Assess strategic competition through dynamic resilience, adaptation, and LoCT distance rather than aggregate capability metrics alone.

figures.title: Dynamic LoCT Positioning in U.S.–China Competition (54-Day Observation Window)
figures.caption: The central radar reports phase-averaged LoCT distance across five strategic stages, while the outer orbit tracks day-level trajectories. The U.S. trajectory reflects progressive threshold compression under sustained multi-theater pressure, whereas China maintains a comparatively stable LoCT distance consistent with a lower-exposure operating configuration.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on the MCCM framework.
figures.note: This figure is conceptual and analytical. It is derived from structured assumptions regarding systemic stress, coupling intensity, and adaptive behavior, and does not represent a direct empirical measurement series.

figures.title: Structural Comparison of U.S.–China Systemic Regimes
figures.caption: Compares the United States and China across operating mode, stress profile, LoCT behavior, and risk structure, showing volatility-driven U.S. dynamics and accumulation-driven China dynamics.
figures.source: Author’s analysis based on the MCCM framework.
figures.note: LoCT = loss-of-control threshold. The figure summarizes contrasting systemic configurations described in Section 4.

tables.title: Structural Comparison of U.S.–China Systemic Regimes
tables.description: Visual table comparing U.S. and China systemic regimes by operating mode, stress profile, LoCT behavior, and risk structure.

rows.United States: High-pressure, outward-oriented; engaged across multiple theaters and domains with global reach and alliance commitments.
rows.China: Controlled, low-exposure; limits direct involvement and manages external engagement selectively.

rows.United States: Distributed across domains; stress spreads across military, economic, political, and alliance systems.
rows.China: Concentrated and internalized; stress is absorbed within internal systems and tightly managed.

rows.United States: Gradual compression over time; LoCT distance narrows steadily as stress and coupling intensify.
rows.China: Relative stability with wider buffer; LoCT distance remains more stable with a larger buffer.

rows.United States: Volatility-driven dynamics; higher volatility and oscillation increase risk of shock amplification and threshold compression.
rows.China: Accumulation-driven dynamics; risk builds gradually within the system and may emerge suddenly.

tables.source: Author’s analysis based on the MCCM framework.
